fields.Identifying_Data_Chief_Complaint: 12-year-old male brought by mother for fear of throwing up affecting eating.
fields.Subjective: Patient is a 12-year-old male presenting with significant fear of vomiting that has led to restrictive eating over past three months. He reports anxiety about eating foods that might cause stomach upset and checks expiration dates obsessively. He denies any thoughts of self-harm or suicidal ideation. Patient states the fear began after he had stomach virus and vomited at school, which was embarrassing. He now avoids school cafeteria and eats only familiar foods prepared at home. Weight is down 4 pounds but still within normal range. He denies body image concerns and states he wants to eat more but is afraid. Continues to attend school and maintain friendships but anxiety about eating is pervasive. Mother notes he carries anti-nausea medication everywhere and asks frequently if food is safe. (ED Physician Dr. Thomas Anderson, DO)
fields.Diagnostic_Impression: Specific phobia (emetophobia); avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder, moderate.
fields.Psychosocial_Context: Lives with both parents and older sister; fear of vomiting following embarrassing public incident; weight loss but still in healthy range; anxious about food safety; maintains peer relationships; recognizes fear is irrational.
Assessment_and_Plan: Emetophobia with resulting food restriction and weight loss; weight still adequate but concerning trend.
Protective factors: weight in normal range, wants to improve, supportive family, recognizes irrationality of fears, no suicidal ideation.
Referral for cognitive behavioral therapy with exposure-based approach to vomiting fears.
Nutritionist consultation to ensure adequate nutrition during treatment.
Consider SSRI if anxiety does not improve with behavioral intervention.
Gradual exposure to feared foods and situations.
Remove safety behaviors including anti-nausea medication and checking behaviors.
Close monitoring of weight; weekly weigh-ins initially.
Outpatient follow-up in 1 week; escalate to eating disorder program if weight loss continues.
fields.Author: Dr. Thomas Anderson, DO (ED Physician)
